name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَقُرْءَانٍ مُّبِينٍ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran. (1)
```

```arabic
رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ (٢)
```

```english
Much will the faithless wish that they had been Muslims. (2)
```

```arabic
ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا۟ وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ وَيُلْهِهِمُ ٱلْأَمَلُ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
```

```english
Leave them to eat and enjoy and to be diverted by longings. Soon they will know. (3)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ (٤)
```

```english
We did not destroy any town but that it had a known term. (4)
```

```arabic
مَّا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (٥)
```

```english
No nation can advance its time nor can it defer it. (5)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِى نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٦)
```

```english
They said, ‘O you, to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy. (6)
```

```arabic
لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٧)
```

```english
Why do you not bring us the angels should you be truthful?!’ (7)
```

```arabic
مَا نُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ (٨)
```

```english
We do not send down the angels except with due reason, and then they will not be granted any respite. (8)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (٩)
```

```english
Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah encompasses discourse regarding the disbelievers' mockery of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), their accusing him of madness (الجنون), and their dismissal of the Noble Quran as the ramblings of the insane. Thus, it contains consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), commanding him to be patient, steadfast, and to overlook them. It serves to comfort his noble soul while providing both warning (إنذار) and glad tidings (تبشير).

It is a Meccan Surah (مكية), as evidenced by its verses. It is narrated in *al-Majma‘* from al-Hasan that he excepted His saying: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَاكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ الْمَثَانِي (Certainly We have given you the seven [oft-repeated verses] of the dual [revelation]) [15:87], and His saying: كَمَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَى الْمُقْتَسِمِينَ الَّذِينَ جَعَلُوا الْقُرْآنَ عِضِينَ (As We sent down on the dividers, who have broken the Quran into fragments) [15:90-91]. The critique of this view will follow.

The Surah also includes the saying of the Exalted: فَاصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists) [15:94]. This verse is applicable to what history records: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained concealed at the beginning of his mission (البعثة) for three, four, or five years, not announcing his call due to the severity of the situation. He would only invite individuals from whom he hoped for faith (الإيمان), calling them secretly and confiding the message to them until his Lord permitted him and commanded him to announce his call publicly.

This is supported by the narrations (الروايات المأثورة) transmitted through both Shi‘i and Sunni chains: that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained concealed at the start of his mission for years, not manifesting his call to the general public until Allah the Exalted revealed to him: فَاصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ إِنَّا كَفَيْنَاكَ الْمُسْتَهْزِئِينَ (So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists. Indeed We will suffice you against the mockers) [15:94-95]. Thereupon, he went out to the people and manifested the call. Accordingly, the Surah is Meccan, revealed at the beginning of the public call.

Among the most brilliant Quranic verses (غرر الآيات) in this Surah, which contain profound truths, is the saying of the Exalted: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ (There is not a thing but its treasures are with Us) [15:21], and His saying: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ (Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it) [15:9].

**His saying, the Exalted**: الر تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ وَقُرْآنٍ مُّبِينٍ (Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran) [15:1]. The demonstrative pronoun refers to the noble Quranic verses; thus, the "Book" (الكتاب) refers to the Quran. The use of the indefinite form for "Quran" (وقرآن) is to indicate the greatness of its status and the magnificence of its affair, just as the use of "these" (تلك) — *which is for distant objects* — serves the same purpose.

The meaning is: These verses, lofty in station and sublime in degree, which We reveal to you, are the signs of the Divine Book and the signs of a Quran of magnificent status, a criterion (فاصل) between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), contrary to what the disbelievers accuse it of when they mockingly charge you with madness regarding the speech of Allah.

It is possible that the "Book" refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) — *the celestial record of all divine decrees* — for the Quran is from it and within it. The Exalted said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (This is indeed a noble Quran, in a protected Book) [56:77-78], and He said: بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَّجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍ (Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet) [85:21-22]. Thus, His saying: تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ وَقُرْآنٍ مُّبِينٍ (These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran) would be like a summary of His saying: وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise) [43:2-4].

**His saying, the Exalted**: رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ (Much will the faithless wish that they had been Muslims) [15:2]. This is a preamble to what will be addressed regarding their saying to the Prophet: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy!) [15:6]. It indicates that they will regret their state of disbelief (الكفر) and will wish for submission (الإسلام) to Allah and faith in His Book on a day when there is no way for them to attain that.

Thus, in His saying: رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ (Much will [they] wish), the intended meaning is the "wish of longing" (ودادة التمني), not absolute liking or love. The evidence for this is His saying in describing this wishing: لَوْ كَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ (that they had been Muslims). The words "if" (لو) and "had been" (كانوا) indicate that their wishing is a longing for their past state regarding what they missed and what shall never return to them; for there is no submission (الإسلام) except while they are in this world.

The verse indicates that those who disbelieved will regret their disbelief and wish they had been Muslims after the carpet of worldly life has been folded away.

**His saying, the Exalted**: ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا وَيُلْهِهِمُ الْأَمَلُ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (Leave them to eat and enjoy and to be diverted by [long] hope: soon they will know) [15:3]. Diversion (الإلهاء) means pure distraction and preoccupation. It is said, "such a thing diverted (ألهاه) him from such a thing," meaning it occupied him and made him forget its mention.

His saying: ذَرْهُمْ يَأْكُلُوا وَيَتَمَتَّعُوا وَيُلْهِهِمُ الْأَمَلُ (Leave them to eat and enjoy and to be diverted by [long] hope) is a command to stay one's hand from them and leave them to the falsehood they are in. It is an allusion (كناية) to the prohibition of arguing with them or presenting proofs against them to establish this truth—namely, that they will soon desire Islam and wish for it when there is no way to attain it or rectify what was missed. His saying: فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (soon they will know) stands as the justification (التعليل) for the command; meaning: leave them, do not argue with them, and do not debate them, for there is no need for that, as they will soon know it, for the truth (الحق) will inevitably manifest.

The verse contains a reproach (تعريض) against them: that they have no goal in their lives except eating, enjoying material pleasures, and being distracted by hopes and fantasies. They have no logic except the logic of cattle and mute beasts (الحيوان العجم). Thus, it is fitting that they be left to what they are in, and that the true proofs—based on sound intellect (العقل السليم) and human logic—not be presented to them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: وَمَا أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ (We have not destroyed any town but that it had a known decree) [15:4], until the end of the two verses, is a confirmation and emphasis of His saying in the previous verse: فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (soon they will know). According to the context (السياق) and meaning: leave them, for they will not submit in this worldly life; rather, they will only desire Islam after their appointed time (أجلهم) arrives and destruction descends upon them. People have no choice in this; rather, for every nation (أمة), there is a known decree (كتاب معلوم) with Allah, in which their term is written; they can neither advance it nor delay it by an hour.

In these two verses, there is an indication that a nation (الأمة) of humans has a "book" (كتاب) just as an individual has a book. The Exalted said: وَكُلَّ إِنسَانٍ أَلْزَمْنَاهُ طَائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كِتَابًا يَلْقَاهُ مَنشُورًا (We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open) [17:13].

**His saying, the Exalted**: وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (And they said, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy!’) [15:6]. This speech is delivered in the manner of mockery (الاستهزاء). For this reason, they addressed him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) not by his name, but by the description of the "Reminder" (الذكر) being sent down upon him, as he used to claim. They used the passive verb (نُزِّلَ) — *was sent down* — to indicate that the sender is unknown to them, and they have no reliance or trust in his claim that Allah the Exalted is the one who sent it. Describing him as "the one to whom the Reminder was sent down," as well as naming what was sent down "a Reminder" (ذكراً), is all part of the mockery, just as their saying: إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (you are indeed crazy) is an accusation and a denial (تكذيب).

**His saying, the Exalted**: لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِالْمَلَائِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ (Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful?) [15:7]. The term *law-ma* (لو ما) is like *halla* (هلا), used for incitement (التحضيض); meaning: why do you not bring us the angels if you are truthful in the claim of prophethood (النبوة), so they may testify to the truth of your claim and warn alongside you? It is close in meaning to their saying, as narrated by Allah: لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيرًا (Why was not an angel sent down to him, to be a warner with him?) [25:7].

The reason for their proposal (اقتراح) to the prophets to bring angels and manifest them was their belief that humanity is a material entity (كينونة مادية) submerged in the filth of lust and anger, having no affinity with the celestial world, which is pure luminosity (نورانية) and sanctity (طهارة). Thus, whoever claimed a type of connection with that spiritual world (العالم الروحاني) must bring some of its inhabitants from the noble angels to confirm his claim and assist him in his call.

Furthermore, the angels, according to the pagans (الوثنيين), were deities (آلهة) besides Allah, the Glorified. Thus, calling them to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — meant that these deities were excluded from intercession (الشفاعة) and worship (العبادة) by the command of Allah, who is the God of gods. Since there was no evidence for this that they recognized, they demanded the angels descend, recognize him, and confirm the prophethood.

**His saying, the Exalted**: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ (We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite) [15:8]. This is the response to their proposal to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he bring them the angels so they might believe him. The gist of the response is that the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah's action* — proceeds by veiling His angels from them behind the curtains of the Unseen (الغيب). If He were to send them down and manifest them to them upon their proposal, that would be a celestial sign (آية سماوية) breaking the natural order (خارقة للعادة) revealed in response to their demand. It is the nature of a miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة) revealed upon the proposal of the people that it is followed by the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) and certain destruction (الهلاك القطعي) if they do not believe in it. Since these stubborn disbelievers are not believers, it would mean their destruction.

In short, if Allah were to send down the angels while the situation is as it is—they proposing a decisive sign that manifests the truth and removes falsehood—He would send them down with the "decisive truth" (بالحق الفاصل) that distinguishes, and they would not then be granted respite (منظرين); rather, they would be destroyed and their remnants cut off. This is the summary of what some have mentioned.

It has also been said: The "truth" (الحق) in the verse refers to death (الموت). The meaning would be: We do not send down the angels upon people except accompanied by the truth, which is death, and they would not then be granted respite. It is as if this is taken from the saying of the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ (The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day) [25:22].

It has also been said: The "truth" refers to the message (الرسالة); meaning: We do not send down the angels except with revelation (الوحي) and the message. It is as if this is taken from such verses as: قَدْ جَاءَكُمُ الرَّسُولُ بِالْحَقِّ (The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth) [4:170] and His saying: فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ (They certainly denied the truth when it came to them) [6:5].

These are the views mentioned in the interpretation of the verse, and there are others mentioned in various commentaries (التفاسير). All of them are not without flaw, which is that none of them fits the restriction (الحصر) found in His saying: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ (We do not send down the angels except with the truth). For the descent of angels is not restricted to the punishment of extirpation alone, nor to death alone, nor to revelation and the message alone. Directing the verse toward one of these three meanings for "truth" requires restricting it with many conditions that the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse rejects, as is apparent to whoever reviews the aforementioned views.

It is possible to establish the meaning of the verse by seeking aid from contemplation (التدبر) of other verses: that the realm of material life—meaning this worldly existence (النشأة الدنيوية)—is a realm where truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل) are mixed, without the truth being purely manifest in all its properties and effects, as indicated by His saying: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ (Thus does Allah draw the parable of the truth and the falsehood) [13:17]. We have already detailed this discourse. Thus, nothing of the truth appears in it except that it carries some ambiguity (اللبس) and doubt, as confirmed by an induction (استقراء) of the cases we have encountered throughout our lives. A witness to this is His saying: وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَاهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَاهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ (Had We made him an angel, We would surely have made him a man, and We would surely have confused for them what they are confusing) [6:9]. This realm is the realm of trial (الامتحان) and choice (الاختيار), and there is no choice except with the possibility of truth being confused with falsehood, and good being mixed with evil, such that man stands at the crossroads of two paths (النجدين). He then infers good and evil by their effects and signs, and subsequently chooses what he deserves of felicity (السعادة) or wretchedness (الشقاوة).

As for the world of the angels (عالم الملائكة) and the realm of their existence, it is the world of truth (عالم الحق), unmixed with any falsehood, as indicated by His saying: لَّا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ مَا أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ (they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they carry out what they are commanded) [66:6], and His saying: بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ (Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command) [21:26-27].

The implication of the verses and what is in their meaning is that they are, in themselves, noble creatures and pure, luminous existences (وجودات طاهرة نورانية), sanctified from deficiency and blemish. They do not admit evil or wretchedness, nor is there in them the possibility of corruption, disobedience, or shortcoming. Thus, this material system—built on the basis of possibility (الإمكان), choice, and the permissibility of both righteousness and corruption, obedience and disobedience, felicity and wretchedness—does not govern them. A comprehensive discussion on this will come in its appropriate place, God-willing.

It will also come that man has no way to this "realm of truth" (الظرف الحق) as long as he is deeply immersed in this material world, entangled in the depths of lusts and whims—like the people of disbelief and transgression—except by the dissolution of their world and their exit to the world of truth, its manifestation to them, and the removal of the veil (انكشاف الغطاء) from them, as indicated by His saying: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today) [50:22]. This is the world that is called, in relation to man, the Hereafter (آخرة).

Thus, it becomes clear that the manifestation of the world of angels to people deeply immersed in the world of matter is contingent upon the change of the realm and the transition from this world to the Hereafter, which is death—except for the chosen ones among the servants of Allah and His friends (أولياؤه), who are purified from the filth of sins and are constant in the courtyard of His proximity. They possess the aptitude to witness the Unseen (الغيب) while they are in the world of the manifest (عالم الشهادة), such as the prophets (عليه‌السلام).

Perhaps what we have presented is the meaning of His saying: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ (We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite). For they proposed the descent of the angels so they might witness them in their original forms to believe; and this state is not prepared for them except through death, as the Exalted said: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ (Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why were the angels not sent down to us?’) — until He said — يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا (The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’ We shall set out to [examine] what works they have done, and We shall turn them into scattered dust) [25:21-23].

Both meanings have come together in the saying of the Exalted: وَقَالُوا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ وَلَوْ أَنزَلْنَا مَلَكًا لَّقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لَا يُنظَرُونَ وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَاهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَاهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ (They say, ‘Why has not an angel been sent down to him?’ Had We sent down an angel, the matter would surely have been decided, and then they would not have been granted any respite. Had We made him an angel, We would surely have made him a man, and We would surely have confused for them what they are confusing) [6:8-9]. The Exalted says: If We had sent down the angels to him as a miraculous sign confirming the prophethood, its necessary consequence would be the judgment (القضاء) upon them and their destruction. And if We had assigned the prophethood and message to an angel, its necessary consequence would be that We fashion him in the form of a human man, and that We place him in a position that admits ambiguity (اللبس); for the message is one of the means of Divine trial and testing (الامتحان والابتلاء الإلهي), and there is no trial except with that which admits felicity and wretchedness, success and failure, and through which both salvation and destruction are possible. If the message were attained through that which compels the intellects to faith and forces the souls to acceptance and certainty, all of that would be nullified.

**His saying, the Exalted**: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ (Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it) [15:9]. The beginning of the verse is phrased in the manner of restriction (الحصر). The apparent context is that the restriction refers to what was mentioned of their rejection of the Quran as the ramblings of madness, and that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a madman whose actions are of no account and who is not to be restrained; and to their proposal that he bring them the angels to confirm him in his call and that the Quran is a true celestial book.

The meaning—based on this, and Allah knows best—is that this Reminder (الذكر) was not brought by you from yourself such that they could frustrate it and nullify it through their stubbornness and the severity of their assault, and you would then be burdened with its preservation and be unable. Nor is it sent down from the angels such that it would be in need of their descent and their confirmation of it. Rather, *We* sent down this Reminder in a gradual revelation (إنزالاً تدريجياً), and *We* are its preservers (لحافظون) in its quality as a Reminder, by virtue of the complete care We have for it.

Thus, it is a living, eternal Reminder, protected from dying or being forgotten from its root; protected from addition (الزيادة) that would nullify its being a Reminder; protected from deficiency (النقص) likewise; and protected from change (التغيير) in its form and context such that its quality as a Reminder of Allah, manifesting the truths of His knowledge, would be altered.

The verse indicates that the Book of Allah is preserved from all types of alteration (التحريف) from the perspective of its being a Reminder of Allah, the Glorified; thus, it is a living, eternal Reminder.

Similar to this verse in indicating that the Mighty Book is preserved by the preservation of Allah, protected from alteration and interference in any way from the perspective of its being a Reminder of Him, the Glorified, is the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ (Indeed those who misbelieve in the Reminder when it comes to them... and indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a revelation from the All-wise, the All-laudable) [41:41-42].

It has become clear from what preceded that the definite article (ال) in "the Reminder" (الذكر) is for the specific mention (للعهد الذكري), and that the intended meaning of the description "preservers" (لحافظون) is the future, as is apparent from the active participle (اسم الفاعل). By this, the objection that is sometimes raised against the verse is refuted—namely, that if it indicated the negation of alteration from the Quran because it is a "Reminder," it would also indicate its negation from the Torah and the Gospel, as each of them is a "Reminder," while His speech, the Exalted, is explicit regarding the occurrence of alteration in them.

This is because the verse, by the evidence of the context, only indicates the preservation of the Reminder which is the Quran after its revelation forever. There is no indication in it that being a "Reminder" is the sole cause (علية) for Divine preservation such that the ruling would revolve around it wherever it is found.

We shall fully explore the research pertaining to this matter, God-willing.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain to his father, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from ‘Umar ibn Udhaynah, from Rifa‘ah, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: **إذا كان يوم القيامة نادى مناد من عند الله : لا يدخل الجنة إلا مسلم ـ فيومئذ يود الذين كفروا لو كانوا مسلمين** — "When the Day of Resurrection comes, a caller will call out from Allah: 'None shall enter Paradise except a Muslim.' On that day, those who disbelieved will wish they had been Muslims." Then he said: **« ذرهم يأكلوا ويتمتعوا ويلههم الأمل » أي شغلهم « فسوف يعلمون »** — "'Leave them to eat and enjoy and to be diverted by [long] hope'—meaning it occupied them—'soon they will know.'"

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Ata’ al-Makki, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), a similar interpretation of the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a sound chain from Jabir ibn ‘Abdillah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **إن ناسا من أمتي يعذبون بذنوبهم ـ فيكونون في النار ما شاء الله أن يكونوا ـ ثم يعيرهم أهل الشرك فيقولون : ما نرى ما كنتم فيه من تصديقكم نفعكم ـ فلا يبقى موحد إلا أخرجه الله تعالى من النار** — "Indeed, some people from my nation will be punished for their sins—remaining in the Fire as long as Allah wills them to remain—then the people of polytheism will taunt them, saying: 'We do not see that the confirmation [of faith] you were upon has benefited you.' Thereupon, no monotheist (موحد) will remain but that Allah the Exalted will bring him out of the Fire." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ (Much will the faithless wish that they had been Muslims).

I say: This meaning is narrated through other chains from Abu Musa al-Ash‘ari, Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, and Anas ibn Malik from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Shahin in *al-Sunnah* narrated from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **إن أصحاب الكبائر من موحدي الأمم ـ كلها الذين ماتوا على كبائرهم ـ غير نادمين ولا تائبين من دخل منهم جهنم لا تزرق أعينهم ، ولا تسود وجوههم ، ولا يقرنون بالشياطين ولا يغلون بالسلاسل ، ولا يجرعون الحميم ، ولا يلبسون القطران ـ حرم الله أجسادهم على الخلود من أجل التوحيد ، وصورهم على النار من أجل السجود ـ** — "Indeed, the perpetrators of major sins (أصحاب الكبائر) among the monotheists of all nations—those who died upon their major sins without being regretful or repentant—whoever among them enters Hell, their eyes shall not turn blue, their faces shall not turn black, they shall not be shackled with devils, nor bound in chains, nor made to drink boiling water, nor clothed in pitch. Allah has forbidden their bodies from eternity [in the Fire] for the sake of monotheism (التوحيد), and [forbidden] their faces to the Fire for the sake of prostration (السجود)."

**فمنهم من تأخذه النار إلى قدميه ، ومنهم من تأخذه النار إلى عقبيه ، ومنهم من تأخذه النار إلى فخذيه ، ومنهم من تأخذه النار إلى حجزته ، ومنهم من تأخذه النار إلى عنقه ـ على قدر ذنوبهم وأعمالهم ، ومنهم من يمكث فيها شهرا ثم يخرج منها ، ومنهم من يمكث فيها سنة ثم يخرج منها ، وأطولهم فيها مكثا بقدر الدنيا منذ يوم خلقت إلى أن تفنى.** — "Among them are those whom the Fire takes up to their feet, some to their heels, some to their thighs, some to their waist, and some to their neck—according to their sins and deeds. Some remain therein for a month then come out, some for a year then come out, and the one who remains the longest remains for the duration of the world from the day it was created until it perishes."

**فإذا أراد الله أن يخرجهم منها ـ قالت اليهود والنصارى ومن في النار ـ من أهل الأديان والأوثان لمن في النار من أهل التوحيد : آمنتم بالله وكتبه ورسله فنحن وأنتم اليوم في النار سواء ـ فيغضب الله لهم غضبا لم يغضبه لشيء فيما مضى ـ فيخرجهم إلى عين بين الجنة والصراط ـ فينبتون فيها نبات الطراثيث في حميل السيل ـ ثم يدخلون الجنة مكتوب في جباههم : هؤلاء الجهنميون عتقاء الرحمن ـ فيمكثون في الجنة ما شاء الله أن يمكثوا ـ.** — "When Allah intends to bring them out, the Jews, Christians, and those in the Fire from the people of [other] religions and idols will say to those in the Fire from the people of monotheism: 'You believed in Allah, His Books, and His Messengers, yet today we and you are equal in the Fire.' Thereupon, Allah will become angry for them with an anger He has never felt for anything in the past. He will bring them out to a spring between Paradise and the Path (الصراط), and they will grow therein like the growth of desert stalks (الطراثيث) in the silt of a flood (حميل السيل). Then they will enter Paradise with it written on their foreheads: 'These are the Hell-dwellers, the emancipated ones of the All-Beneficent.' They will remain in Paradise as long as Allah wills them to remain."

**ثم يسألون الله تعالى أن يمحو ذلك الاسم عنهم ـ فيبعث الله ملكا فيمحوه ـ ثم يبعث الله ملائكة معهم مسامير من نار ـ فيطبقونها على من بقي فيها يسمرونها بتلك المسامير ـ فينساهم الله على عرشه ، ويشتغل عنهم أهل الجنة بنعيمهم ولذاتهم ، وذلك قوله : (*رُبَما يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ*).** — "Then they will ask Allah the Exalted to erase that name from them, so Allah will send an angel to erase it. Then Allah will send angels with nails of fire; they will close it [Hell] upon those who remain in it, nailing it with those nails. Then Allah will 'forget' them upon His Throne, and the people of Paradise will be occupied away from them by their bliss and pleasures. That is His saying: رُّبَمَا يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ كَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ (Much will the faithless wish that they had been Muslims)."

I say: *Al-Turthuth* (الطرثوث) is a plant, and *hamil al-sayl* (حميل السيل) is its debris. Narrations with similar content have been transmitted through Shi‘i chains.

In the same source, Ahmad and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa‘id: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) planted a stick in front of him, another by his side, and another further away. He said: **أتدرون ما هذا؟** — "Do you know what this is?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: **فإن هذا الإنسان وهذا أجله وهذا أمله ـ فيتعاطى الأمل فيختلجه الأجل دون ذلك.** — "This is man, this is his appointed term (أجله), and this is his hope (أمله). He reaches for hope, but the term snatches him away before that."

I say: Something close to its meaning is narrated through chains from Anas from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Majma‘*, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), that he said: **إن أخوف ما أخاف عليكم اثنان : اتباع الهوى ، وطول الأمل ، فإن اتباع الهوى يصد عن الحق ، وطول الأمل ينسي الآخرة.** — "Indeed, the two things I fear most for you are: following whims (اتباع الهوى) and long hope (طول الأمل). For following whims bars one from the truth, and long hope causes one to forget the Hereafter."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ (We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite), he said: He (peace be upon him) said: **لو أنزلنا بالملائكة لم ينظروا وهلكوا.** — "If We had sent down the angels, they would not have been granted respite and would have perished."

## A Discourse on the Quran Being Protected from Alteration in Several Chapters:

### Chapter 1

It is among the historical necessities (ضروريات التاريخ) that the Arabian Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came approximately fourteen centuries ago, claimed prophethood, rose for the call, and a nation of Arabs and others believed in him. He brought a Book he called the Quran, attributing it to his Lord, containing the sum of knowledge (المعارف) and the universals of the Sharia (كليات الشريعة) to which he was calling. He used to challenge others with it and considered it a sign (آية) of his prophethood. The Quran present today in our hands is, in general, the same Quran he brought and recited to the people contemporary to him—meaning it has not perished from its root such that it was entirely lost and then another book resembling it in its composition (نظمه) or not resembling it was fabricated, attributed to him, and became famous among people as the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

These are matters about which no one doubts except one whose understanding is afflicted. None of the researchers on the issue of alteration (التحريف)—whether opponents or proponents—has entertained such a possibility.

Rather, some who spoke of it—whether opponent or proponent—only entertained the possibility of the addition of something minor, like a phrase or a verse (1), or the deficiency or change in a phrase or verse regarding its words or its declension (إعرابها). As for the bulk of the Divine Book, it remains as it was in the era of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), neither perished nor lost.

__________________
(1) Like the saying of some non-adherents to Islam that the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُم مَّيِّتُونَ (You will surely die, and they [too] will surely die) [39:30] was an insertion by Abu Bakr; he inserted it when he heard ‘Umar brandishing his sword and threatening to kill whoever said the Prophet had died, so he recited it to ‘Umar and turned him away.

Furthermore, we find the Quran challenging with attributes that apply to all its verses. We find the Quran in our hands—meaning what is between the two covers—possessing those attributes it challenged with, without any of them changing or being lost.

We find it challenging with eloquence (البلاغة) and lucidity (الفصاحة), and we find what is in our hands encompassing that wondrous and innovative composition (النظم) which nothing of the speech of the eloquent—preserved from them and narrated from them of poetry, prose, sermons, letters, dialogues, or otherwise—can equal or resemble. This composition is present in all the verses, as a Book consistent (متشابها) and oft-repeated (مثاني), at which the skins and hearts shiver.

We find it challenging with His saying: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا (Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [4:82], regarding the absence of discrepancy in it. We find the Quran in our hands fulfilling that in the best and most complete manner. There is no ambiguity or flaw that appears in a verse but that another verse removes it; and there is no discrepancy or contradiction that the initial thought imagines in a part but that there is something there to repel and explain it.

We find it challenging with other things whose understanding is not restricted to the people of the Arabic language, as in His saying: قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا (Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn get together to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they helped one another’) [17:88], and His saying: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَمَا هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ (It is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest) [86:13-14]. Then we find the Quran in our hands providing the full explanation of the explicit truth (صريح الحق) in which there is no doubt, and guiding to the furthest extent to which the intellect is guided regarding the principles of true knowledge (أصول المعارف الحقيقية), the universals of primordial laws (كليات الشرائع الفطرية), and the details of moral virtues (الفضائل الخلقية), without us encountering in it any deficiency or flaw, or finding any contradiction or slip. Rather, we find all the sciences, despite their breadth and multiplicity, alive with a single life, governed by a single spirit, which is the principle of all Quranic knowledge and the root to which everything ends and returns: monotheism (التوحيد). To it, everything returns through analysis (التحليل), and it returns to each of them through synthesis (التركيب).

We find it diving into the reports of the past prophets and their nations, and we find what we have of the speech of Allah narrating their stories and detailing them in a manner befitting the purity of religion and suitable for the sanctity of the station of prophethood and its exclusivity to servitude and obedience. Whenever we apply a Quranic story to its equivalent in what is reported in the Two Testaments, this becomes most clearly manifest.

We find it providing verses regarding epics (الملاحم) and informing of future events in many verses, either explicitly or by allusion. Then we find them in the Quran in our hands, upon that condition, truthful and confirmed.

We find it describing itself with pure and beautiful attributes, such as describing itself as a light (نور) and a guide (هاد) that leads to a straight path and to the creed (الملة) which is most upright. We find the Quran in our hands lacking nothing of that, nor neglecting any detail of guidance and direction.

Among the most comprehensive descriptions the Quran mentions for itself is that it is a "Reminder" (ذكر) of Allah. For it reminds of Him, the Exalted, insofar as it is a living, eternal sign (آية) pointing to Him, and insofar as it describes Him by His Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes. It describes His Sunnah in creation and origination; it describes His angels, Books, and Messengers; it describes His laws and rulings; and it describes that to which the affair of creation ends, which is the Return (المعاد) and the return of all to Him, the Glorified, along with the details of what the affair of people will result in of felicity and wretchedness, Paradise and the Fire.

In all of that, it is a Reminder of Allah. This is what the Quran intends by the absolute statement that it is a "Reminder." We find the Quran in our hands lacking nothing of the meaning of "Reminder."

Because "Reminder" is among the most comprehensive attributes in pointing to the affairs of the Quran, it was referred to as "the Reminder" in the verses where He informed of His preservation of the Quran from nullification, change, and alteration, such as His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي آيَاتِنَا لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَا أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِي آمِنًا يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ اعْمَلُوا مَا شِئْتُمْ إِنَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ (Indeed those who profane Our signs are not hidden from Us. Is he who is cast into the Fire better, or he who comes safe on the Day of Resurrection? Do what you will; indeed He sees best what you do. Indeed those who misbelieve in the Reminder when it comes to them... and indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a revelation from the All-wise, the All-laudable) [41:40-42]. He, the Exalted, mentioned that the Quran, from the perspective of its being a Reminder, is not overcome by falsehood, nor does it enter it now or in the future, neither by nullification, nor by abrogation, nor by change or alteration that would cause its quality as a Reminder to vanish from it.

And like His saying: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ (Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it) [15:9]. He used "the Reminder" and "preservation" (الحفظ) in an absolute sense. Thus, the Quran is preserved by the preservation of Allah from every addition, deficiency, and change in wording or arrangement that would remove it from being a Reminder or nullify its being a Reminder of Allah, the Glorified, in any way.

It is a fatuous claim to return the pronoun "it" (له) to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), for it is refuted by the context. The polytheists only mocked the Prophet because of the Quran which he claimed was revealed to him, as indicated by His previous saying: وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ (And they said, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy!’) [15:6], the interpretation of which has passed.

It has become clear from what we have detailed that the Quran which Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and described as a Reminder is preserved as it was revealed, protected by Divine protection from addition, deficiency, and change, as Allah promised His Prophet therein.

The summary of the argument is that Allah revealed the Quran to His Prophet and described it in many verses with specific attributes. If it had changed in any of these attributes through addition, deficiency, or an effective change in wording or arrangement, the effect of that attribute would have certainly vanished. However, we find the Quran in our hands possessing the effects of those enumerated attributes in the most complete and best possible manner. Therefore, no alteration has occurred in it that strips it of any of its attributes. Thus, what is in our hands of it is the very Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). If one were to assume the omission of something from it, or a change in a declension, a letter, or an arrangement, it must be in a matter that does not affect any of its attributes—such as inimitability (الإعجاز), the absence of discrepancy, guidance, luminosity, its being a Reminder, and its authority (الهيمنة) over other celestial books, and so on. This would be like a dropped repeated verse, or a difference in a diacritical point or declension, and the like.

## Chapter 2

The absence of the occurrence of alteration is indicated by the numerous narrations (الأخبار) reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through the chains of both groups [Shi‘a and Sunni], commanding the return to the Quran during trials (الفتن) and for the resolution of complex problems.

Likewise, the *Hadith al-Thaqalayn* (حديث الثقلين), which is mutawatir (متواتر) — *successively narrated by so many that collusion on a lie is impossible* — through the chains of both groups: **( إني تارك فيكم الثقلين ـ كتاب الله وعترتي أهل بيتي ـ ما إن تمسكتم بهما لن تضلوا بعدي أبدا )** — "I am leaving among you the two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny, the people of my house. As long as you hold fast to them, you shall never go astray after me." There is no meaning to the command to hold fast to an altered book and the negation of ever going astray for the one who holds fast to it.

Likewise, the numerous narrations reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) commanding that narrations be presented (عرض) to the Book. What some have mentioned—that this applies only to legal narrations (الأخبار الفقهية), and that it is permissible to maintain the absence of alteration specifically in the verses of rulings (آيات الأحكام) while this does not benefit other verses—is refuted by the fact that the narrations of "presentation" are absolute; thus, restricting them to that is a restriction without a restrictor.

Furthermore, the tone of the narrations of "presentation" is like an explicit statement—or is indeed explicit—that the command to present is only to distinguish truth from falsehood and honesty from lying. It is well known that fabrication (الدس) and forgery (الوضع) are not confined to legal narrations; rather, the motives for fabrication and forgery in the sciences of belief (المعارف الاعتقادية), the stories of the prophets and past nations, and the descriptions of the Origin and the Return are more numerous and abundant. This is supported by the *Isra’iliyyat* (الإسرائيليات) — *narrations of Judeo-Christian origin* — in our hands and what follows their course, where the matter of forgery is most clear and evident.

Likewise, the narrations that encompass the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) holding fast to various Quranic verses in every chapter in accordance with the Quran present with us, even in instances where there are isolated reports (آحاد) of alteration. This is the best witness that the intended meaning in many of the narrations of alteration—from their saying (عليه‌السلام) "thus it was revealed"—is the interpretation according to the revelation (التنزيل) as opposed to the inner meaning (البطن) and esoteric interpretation (التأويل).

Likewise, the narrations reported from the Commander of the Faithful and the other Imams from his progeny (عليه‌السلام) stating that what is in the hands of the people is a Quran revealed from Allah, the Glorified, even if it is other than the codex (المصحف) that ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) compiled, and they did not include him (عليه‌السلام) in the compilation during the time of Abu Bakr nor during the time of ‘Uthman. From this category is their saying (عليه‌السلام) to their followers: **« اقرءوا كما قرأ الناس »** — "Recite as the people recite."

The implication of these narrations is that if the Quran circulating among the people were to differ from what ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) compiled in anything, it would only differ in the arrangement of the Surahs or in the arrangement of some verses whose disruption of arrangement does not affect their meaning in any way, nor the attributes with which Allah, the Glorified, described the Quran revealed from Him such that their effects would be disrupted.

Thus, the sum of these narrations, despite their different categories, indicates with definitive evidence that what is in our hands of the Quran is the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) without losing any of its noble attributes, effects, and blessings.

## Chapter 3

A group of the traditionists (محدثي) of the Shi‘a and the *Hashwiyyah*, and a group of the traditionists of the Sunnis, went toward the occurrence of alteration (التحريف) in the sense of deficiency (النقص) and change in wording or arrangement, but not addition (الزيادة); for no one among the Muslims held that view, as has been said.

They argued for the negation of addition by consensus (الإجماع), and for the occurrence of deficiency and change by many ways.

First: The numerous narrations reported through Shi‘i and Sunni chains indicating the omission of some Surahs and verses, as well as phrases, parts of phrases, words, and letters in the first compilation in which the Quran was assembled during the time of Abu Bakr, and likewise in the second compilation during the time of ‘Uthman, as well as change. These are numerous narrations reported by the Shi‘a in their reliable collections and elsewhere; some have claimed they reach two thousand hadiths. The Sunnis reported them in their *Sahih* collections, such as the *Sahih* of al-Bukhari and Muslim, the *Sunan* of Abu Dawud, al-Nasa’i, Ahmad, and other collections and books of *Tafsir*. Al-Alusi mentioned in his *Tafsir* that they are beyond counting.

This is besides what the codex of ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud differs from the well-known codex in more than sixty places; and what the codex of Ubayy ibn Ka‘b differs from the ‘Uthmanic codex in some thirty places; and the differences between the ‘Uthmanic codices which he had transcribed and sent to the horizons—which were five or seven, sent to Mecca, Syria, Basra, Kufa, Yemen, and Bahrain, while keeping one in Medina. The differences between them reach forty-five letters, and it is said some fifty letters. (1) And besides the difference in arrangement between the ‘Uthmanic codices and the first compilation in the time of Abu Bakr; for Surah al-Anfal was in the first compilation among the *Mathani* (the dual revelation) and Surah Bara’ah among the *Mi’in* (the hundreds), while in the second compilation they were placed among the *Tiwal* (the long ones), as the narration will come.

And besides the difference in the arrangement of Surahs found between the codices of ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud and Ubayy ibn Ka‘b—according to what the narration states—and the ‘Uthmanic codices. And besides the anomalous (شاذة) variant readings reported from the Companions and the Successors. The total number may reach a thousand or exceed it.

The second way: That the intellect judges that if the Quran were scattered, dispersed, and spread among the people, and its compilation was undertaken by someone other than the Infallible (المعصوم), it is habitually impossible for its compilation to be complete and in accordance with reality.

The third way: What both the general and specific [Sunni and Shi‘a] narrated: That ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) secluded himself from the people after the passing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and did not come out except for prayer until he compiled the Quran, then he brought it to the people—and informed them that it was the Quran that Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They rejected it and deemed themselves in no need of it because of what Zayd ibn Thabit had compiled for them. If some of what was in it were not contrary to some of what was in the codex of Zayd, there would have been no reason for his bringing it to them, informing them, and calling them to it. He (عليه‌السلام) was the most knowledgeable of people regarding the Book of Allah after His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he referred the people to it in the mutawatir *Hadith al-Thaqalayn*, and said in the agreed-upon hadith: **« علي مع الحق والحق مع علي »** — "‘Ali is with the truth and the truth is with ‘Ali."

The fourth way: What has been reported in narrations that there will occur in this nation what occurred in the Children of Israel, "step by step and arrow by arrow" (حذو النعل بالنعل والقذة بالقذة). The Children of Israel altered the book of their prophet, as the Noble Quran and the reported narrations explicitly state. Therefore, its equivalent must occur in this nation, so they would alter the Book of their Lord, which is the Noble Quran.

In *Sahih al-Bukhari*, from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **لتتبعن سنن من كان قبلكم شبرا بشبر وذراعا بذراع ـ حتى لو دخلوا جحر ضب لتبعتموه ـ قلنا : يا رسول الله ـ بآبائنا وأمهاتنا اليهود والنصارى؟ قال : فمن؟** — "You shall surely follow the ways of those before you, span by span and cubit by cubit—even if they entered the hole of a lizard, you would follow them." We said: "O Messenger of Allah—by our fathers and mothers—the Jews and the Christians?" He said: "Who else?"

The narration is widespread (مستفيضة), reported in the collections of hadith from several Companions such as Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri—as passed—Abu Hurayrah, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, Ibn ‘Abbas, Hudhayfah, ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, Sahl ibn Sa‘d, ‘Umar ibn ‘Awf, ‘Amr ibn al-‘As, Shaddad ibn Aws, and al-Mustawrid ibn Shaddad in similar wordings.

It is reported and widespread through Shi‘i chains from several Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as in the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **لتركبن سبيل من كان قبلكم حذو النعل بالنعل ـ والقذة بالقذة لا تخطئون طريقهم ـ ولا تخطئ شبر بشبر وذراع بذراع وباع بباع ـ حتى أن لو كان من قبلكم دخل جحر ضب لدخلتموه ـ قالوا : اليهود والنصارى تعني يا رسول الله؟ قال : فمن أعني؟ لتنقضن عرى الإسلام عروة عروة ـ فيكون أول ما تنقضون من دينكم الأمانة وآخره الصلاة.** — "You shall surely ride the path of those before you, step by step and arrow by arrow; you shall not miss their path, nor shall it miss you, span by span, cubit by cubit, and fathom by fathom—even if those before you entered the hole of a lizard, you would enter it." They said: "The Jews and the Christians, you mean, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Who else do I mean? You shall surely break the bonds of Islam bond by bond—the first of what you break of your religion will be trustworthiness (الأمانة) and the last of it will be prayer (الصلاة)."

The response to their deduction using the consensus (إجماع) of the nation to negate the alteration of the Quran by addition is that it is a flawed argument because it is circular (دورية).

The explanation of this: Consensus is not in itself a certain intellectual proof (حجة عقلية يقينية); rather, according to those who consider it valid, it is a religious proof (حجة شرعية). If it yields any belief (اعتقاد), it only yields conjecture (الظن), whether it is "attained" (محصل) or "transmitted" (منقول), contrary to what many of them claim—that attained consensus yields certainty (القطع). This is because the belief that consensus yields does not exceed the sum of the beliefs yielded by individual opinions. A single opinion among agreeing opinions only yields conjecture regarding its correspondence with reality; the addition of a second opinion that agrees with it only yields a strengthening of the conjecture, not certainty, because certainty is a specific, simple belief distinct from conjecture, and is not composed of several conjectures.

Thus, every time an opinion is added to an opinion and agreeing opinions accumulate, the conjecture increases in strength and conjectures accumulate, approaching certainty without being transformed into it, as passed. This is regarding the "attained" (المحصل) consensus, which we attain by tracking all opinions and obtaining each one. As for the "transmitted" (المنقول) from it, which one or two of the people of knowledge and research transmit, the matter is even clearer; it is like isolated narrations (آحاد الروايات), yielding nothing but conjecture if it yields any belief at all.

Thus, consensus is a conjectural religious proof whose evidence for validity among the Sunnis, for example, is his saying (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **( لا تجتمع أمتي على خطإ أو ضلال )** — "My nation shall not gather upon an error or misguidance." Among the Shi‘a, it is the inclusion of the Infallible's opinion among the opinions of those in consensus, or the disclosure of his opinion through theirs in some way.

The validity of consensus, in short, is contingent upon the correctness of prophethood (النبوة), and that is obvious. The correctness of prophethood today is contingent upon the safety of the Quran from alteration that would necessitate the disappearance of the noble attributes of the Quran from it—such as guidance, the decisive word, and especially inimitability (الإعجاز). For there is no living, eternal proof for the specific prophethood of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) other than the Noble Quran being a miraculous sign (آية معجزة). With the possibility of alteration by addition, deficiency, or any other change, there is no trust in any of its verses and contents that it is purely the speech of Allah, and thereby the proof falls and the sign is corrupted. With the fall of the Book of Allah from being a proof, consensus falls from being a proof.

What we presented at the beginning of the discourse—that the existence of the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) within the Quran in our hands is, in general, among the historical necessities—does not benefit here.

This is because the mere inclusion of the actual Quran within what is in our hands does not repel the possibility of addition, deficiency, or any other change in every verse or phrase intended to be used as a proof to establish a requirement.

The response to the first way established for the occurrence of alteration by deficiency and change—which is the one that held fast to the narrations: Firstly, holding fast to the narrations insofar as they are religious proofs (حجة شرعية) involves the same circularity as holding fast to consensus, according to the similar explanation that passed just now.

Thus, nothing remains for the one deducing from them except to hold fast to them insofar as they are historical documents and sources. There is no mutawatir (متواتر) hadith among them, nor any surrounded by definitive indicators that compel the intellect to accept it. Rather, they are isolated reports (آحاد), scattered, dispersed, and differing; some are sound (صحاح) and some are weak (ضعاف) in their chains, and some are deficient in their indication (دلالتها). How rare among them is that which is sound in its chain and complete in its indication.

This type, despite its rarity and scarcity, is not safe from forgery (الوضع) and fabrication (الدس). For the infiltration of *Isra’iliyyat* and the fabrications and forgeries attached to them among our narrations is something that cannot be denied. There is no validity (حجية) in a report that is not safe from fabrication and forgery.

Overlooking that, they mention verses and Surahs that do not resemble the Quranic composition (النظم القرآني) in any way. Overlooking all of that, they are contrary to the Book and thus rejected. As for what we mentioned—that most of them are weak in their chains—this is known by referring to their chains; they are *mursal* (مراسيل) — *missing the companion in the chain* — or broken in their chains, or weak. Those safe from these defects are the fewest of the few.

As for what we mentioned—that some are deficient in their indication—many of the verses cited in them as being from the "altered" ones are of the nature of interpretation (التفسير) and mentioning the meaning of the verses, not the narration of the text of the altered verse. This is as in *Rawdat al-Kafi*, from Abu al-Hasan the First: regarding the saying of Allah: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ مَا فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ (Those are they whom Allah knows what is in their hearts, so turn away from them) — **فقد سبقت عليهم كلمة الشقاء ـ وسبق لهم العذاب** — "for the word of wretchedness has preceded against them, and the punishment has preceded for them" — وَقُل لَّهُمْ فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًا بَلِيغًا (and speak to them a far-reaching word concerning themselves) [4:63].

And what is in *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَإِن تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا (If you twist [your testimony] or turn away) [4:135], he said: **« إن تلووا الأمر وتعرضوا عما أمرتم به فإن الله كان بما تعملون خبيرا »** — "'If you twist the matter and turn away from what you were commanded, then indeed Allah is aware of what you do.'" This is besides other narrations of interpretation (التفسير) counted among the reports of alteration.

Attached to this category are the countless narrations referring to the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) counted among the reports of alteration, such as the narrations that mention this verse thus: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ (O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you) — **في علي** — "concerning ‘Ali," while the verse was revealed regarding his right (عليه‌السلام). And what was narrated: That the delegation of Banu Tamim, when they came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), would stand at the door of the chamber and call him to come out to them; so the verse was mentioned regarding them thus: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَاءِ الْحُجُرَاتِ (Indeed those who call you from behind the chambers) — **بنو تميم** — "Banu Tamim," أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (most of them do not exercise their reason) [49:4]. Thus, it was thought that there was an omission in the verse.

Also attached to this category are the countless narrations reported regarding the "flow" (جري) of the Quran and its application (انطباقه), as reported regarding His saying: وَسَيَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا (And those who do wrong shall soon know) — **آل محمد حقهم** — "the Progeny of Muhammad their right," and what was reported of His saying: وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ (And whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle) — **في ولاية علي والأئمة من بعده** — "concerning the guardianship (ولاية) of ‘Ali and the Imams after him," فَقَدْ فَازَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (has certainly achieved a great triumph) [33:71]. These are very numerous.

Also attached to them is that in which the recitation was followed by something of remembrance (الذكر) and supplication (الدعاء), so it was imagined to be from the omitted parts of the Quran, as in *al-Kafi*, from ‘Abd al-‘Aziz ibn al-Muhtadi, who said: I asked al-Rida (peace be upon him) about monotheism (التوحيد), and he said: **كل من قرأ (*قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ*) وآمن بها فقد عرف التوحيد** — "Whoever recites 'Say, He is Allah, the One' and believes in it has known monotheism." He said: [I said] "How should we recite it?" He said: **« كما يقرؤها الناس ـ وزاد فيه كذلك الله ربي كذلك الله ربي »** — "'As the people recite it'—and he added to it: 'Thus is Allah my Lord, thus is Allah my Lord.'"

Among the instances of deficiency in indication is what we find in many of the verses counted as altered: the difference of narrations regarding the wording of the verse, such as those reported regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ (Certainly Allah helped you at Badr, when you were abject) [3:123]. In some of them, the verse is thus: **( ولقد نصركم الله ببدر وأنتم ضعفاء )** — "Certainly Allah helped you at Badr when you were weak," and in some: **« ولقد نصركم الله ببدر وأنتم قليل »** — "Certainly Allah helped you at Badr when you were few."

This variation may perhaps serve as an indication that what is intended is an explanation of the meaning, as seen in the aforementioned verse. This is supported by what has been narrated in some reports from his saying (عليه‌السلام): "It is not permissible to describe them as being abased (أذلة) while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was among them."

At other times, it may be nothing more than a case of contradiction and mutual negation between the narrations, which necessitates their dismissal—such as the "Verse of Stoning" (آية الرجم) mentioned in the narrations of both the elite (الخاصة) —*the Shi‘a*— and the general public (العامة) —*the Sunnis*. In some of these reports, it reads: "If the old man and the old woman commit adultery, stone them certainly, for they have satisfied their desire." In others: "The old man and the old woman, if they commit adultery, stone them certainly, for they have satisfied their desire." In some: "for the pleasure they have satisfied." In others, the end reads: "as an exemplary punishment (نكالاً) from Allah, and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." And in some: "as an exemplary punishment (نكالاً) from Allah, and Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise."

The same applies to the "Verse of the Throne" (آية الكرسي) according to the alleged "revelation" (التنزيل) mentioned in certain narrations. In some, it is recorded as follows:

**اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ لَا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-Sustainer. Neither slumber nor sleep seizes Him. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" — "and what is between them and what is beneath the soil, the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, and He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone" — **مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ** — "Who is it that may intercede with Him" — until His saying — **وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ** — "and He is the All-Exalted, the All-Supreme" — "and praise be to Allah, Lord of all the worlds."

In some reports, it continues until His saying: **هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ** — "they shall remain in it [forever]" — "and praise be to Allah, Lord of all the worlds." In others, it is: **لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" — "and what is between them and what is beneath the soil, the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful," etc. In some: "the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful, the Originator of the heavens and the earth, the Possessor of Majesty and Honor, Lord of the Great Throne." In others: "the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, the All-Mighty, the All-Wise."

As for the claim made by some traditionists (المحدثين) that the variation in these narrations regarding the cited verses is not harmful because they agree on the principle of "alteration" (التحريف) —*the claim that the Quranic text has been changed or tampered with*— it is rejected. Such a claim does not rectify the weakness of the evidence nor the fact that these reports negate one another.

Regarding what we mentioned concerning the prevalence of interpolation (الدس) and fabrication (الوضع) in narrations, no one who reviews the reports transmitted regarding creation (الصنع والإيجاد), the stories of the prophets and nations, the reports concerning the exegesis (تفسير) of verses, and the events of the early Islamic era can doubt it. The most significant matter for the enemies of the religion—who spare no effort in extinguishing its light, quenching its fire, and erasing its traces—is the Noble Quran. It is the fortified refuge (الكهف المنيع) and the firm pillar (الركن الشديد) to which religious knowledge (المعارف الدينية) retreats and within which it is protected. It is the living, eternal testament to the manifesto of prophethood and the core material of the Call. They know well that if the proof of the Quran were invalidated, the matter of prophethood would be corrupted, the system of religion would be disrupted, and not one stone of its structure would remain upon another.

It is astonishing to see those who argue using narrations attributed to the Companions (الصحابة) or to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) for the alteration of the Book of Allah, the Glorified, thereby invalidating its authority (حجية). With the invalidation of the Quran's authority, prophethood goes to waste and religious knowledge becomes idle talk with no effect. What benefit is there in our saying: "A man at such a time in history claimed prophethood and brought the Quran as a miracle (معجزة), but as for him, he has died, and as for his Quran, it has been altered"? Nothing would remain in our hands to support his case except that those who believed in him reached a consensus (أجمعوا) on his truthfulness, and that the Quran he brought was a miracle indicating his prophethood. But consensus is only a proof because that prophet deemed it so, or because it uncovers, for instance, the words of the Imams of his household.

In short, the possibility of interpolation (الدس)—which is very likely and supported by evidence and indications—negates the authority of these narrations and corrupts their credibility. Consequently, they retain neither religious authority (حجية شرعية) nor rational authority (حجية عقلائية), even those with authentic chains of transmission (صحيح الأسناد). The authenticity of a chain and the integrity (عدالة) of the transmitters only negate their intentional lying; it does not negate the interpolation by others into their primary sources (أصول) and compendiums (جوامع) of things they did not actually narrate.

As for our point that the narrations of alteration mention verses and chapters whose composition (نظم) does not resemble the Quranic composition in any way, this is evident to anyone who reviews them. One will encounter much of this, such as the two chapters of *al-Khal‘* and *al-Hafd* narrated through several Sunni chains. The "Chapter of *al-Khal‘*" is: "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. O Allah, we seek Your help and Your forgiveness, and we praise You and do not disbelieve in You, and we cast off and leave those who sin against You." The "Chapter of *al-Hafd*" is: "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. O Allah, You alone we worship, to You we pray and prostrate, and toward You we hasten and serve. We hope for Your mercy and fear Your punishment; indeed, Your punishment shall overtake the disbelievers."

Similarly, what some narrations cite as the "Chapter of *al-Wilayah*" and others are fabricated sayings whose author attempted to imitate the Quranic composition. Consequently, the speech fell outside the familiar Arabic style (الأسلوب) and failed to reach the miraculous divine composition. Thus, it became something the natural disposition (الطبع) finds loathsome and the taste (الذوق) rejects. You may review them yourself to see the truth of our claim and judge that most of those who paid attention to these fabricated and forged chapters and verses were driven only by an intense devotion to narrations and a failure to weigh them against the Book. Had it not been for that, a single glance would have sufficed for them to judge that it is not divine speech.

As for our statement that the narrations of alteration—assuming the authenticity of their chains—contradict the Book, we do not mean merely their contradiction of the literal meaning (ظاهر) of His saying: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ** — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" — and His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** — "and indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" — such that it would be a probabilistic contradiction (مخالفة ظنية) because the literal meanings of words are among the probabilistic evidences. Rather, we mean their contradiction of the definitive indication (الدلالة القطعية) derived from the entirety of the Quran in our hands, as we established in the first argument we presented to negate alteration.

How could it be otherwise? The Quran in our hands is uniform in its parts (متشابه الأجزاء) in its wondrous, miraculous composition. It is sufficient to resolve the apparent discrepancies between its verses and sections. It is neither deficient nor incapable of providing its true knowledge and divine sciences—both universal (الكلية) and particular (الجزئية)—which are interconnected, with their branches following from their roots and their ends reflecting their middles, among other characteristics of the Quranic composition by which Allah has described it.

The answer to the second point is that the claim of "habitual impossibility" (الامتناع العادي) is a clear overstatement. Yes, the intellect (العقل) allows for the possibility that the compilation itself might not correspond to reality unless there are indications (قرائن) pointing to that—and they do exist, as we have previously mentioned. However, for the intellect to judge that it *must* contradict reality, as necessitated by the claim of habitual impossibility, is not the case.

The answer to the third point is that the fact that he (عليه‌السلام) —*Imam ‘Ali*— collected the Quran, brought it to them, and presented it to them does not indicate that what he collected contradicted what they collected in any of the fundamental or secondary religious truths (الحقائق الدينية). At most, there might have been a difference in the order of the chapters (السور) or the verses (الآيات) within chapters that were revealed piecemeal (نجوماً), in a way that does not result in a contradiction regarding any religious truth.

Had it been otherwise, he would have confronted them with arguments and defended his version; he would not have been content with their mere turning away from what he collected and their claim of being self-sufficient without it, as has been narrated from him (عليه‌السلام) in various other instances. It has not been transmitted from him (عليه‌السلام) in any of his narrated arguments that he recited, regarding the matter of his *Wilayah* or anything else, a verse or a chapter indicating it and then rebuked them for omitting or altering it.

Was this for the sake of preserving the unity of the Muslims and avoiding the splitting of the ranks? Such a consideration would only be conceivable after the matter had settled and the people had united upon what was collected for them, not at the time of collection and before it had fallen into the hands of the people and spread throughout the lands.

I wonder, can we really claim that that vast multitude of verses which they claim were omitted—sometimes claiming they reach the thousands—were all regarding the *Wilayah*, or were they hidden and concealed from the general body of Muslims, known only to a tiny few? This, despite the strong motivations and great desire of the Muslims to take the Quran whenever it was revealed and to learn it, and despite the utmost effort of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in conveying it, sending it to the horizons, teaching it, and explaining it. The Quran itself explicitly states this; the Exalted said: **وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ** — "and teach them the Book and wisdom" (62:2), and said: **لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** — "so that you may clarify to the people what has been sent down to them" (16:44). So how was it lost? And where did it go? Some disconnected reports (المراسيل) suggest that more than a third of the Quran—meaning more than two thousand verses—was lost from a single verse at the beginning of Surah al-Nisa’, between His saying: **وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا فِي الْيَتَامَى** — "If you fear that you may not deal justly with the orphans" — and His saying: **فَانْكِحُوا مَا طَابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّسَاءِ** — "then marry [other] women that seem good to you." There are also reports from Sunni chains claiming that Surah al-Bara’ah (Tawbah) used to begin with the *Basmalah* and was equal to Surah al-Baqarah, and that al-Ahzab was greater than al-Baqarah but two hundred verses were lost from it, and so on!

Or were these verses—which these narrations indicate were so numerous—"abrogated in recitation" (منسوخة التلاوة), as mentioned by a group of Sunni exegetes to preserve what was narrated in some of their reports that there are parts of the Quran that Allah caused to be forgotten and whose recitation He abrogated?

What is the meaning of "causing a verse to be forgotten" and "abrogating its recitation"? Was it for the purpose of abrogating the practice (العمل) based on it? If so, what are these abrogated verses that remain in the Quran, such as the verse of charity (الصدقة), the verse of the marriage of the adulterer and adulteress, the verse of the waiting period (العدة), and others? Furthermore, they divide "abrogation of recitation" into that which is abrogated in both recitation and practice, and that which is abrogated in recitation but not in practice, such as the "Verse of Stoning."

Or was it because these verses did not possess some of the attributes of the Word of Allah, such that Allah invalidated them by erasing their mention and removing their trace? If so, they were not part of the "August Book" which "falsehood cannot approach from before it nor from behind it," nor were they free from discrepancy, nor a "decisive word," nor a "guide to the truth and to a straight path," nor a "miracle" by which a challenge is issued, nor, nor... What then is the meaning of the many verses that describe the Quran as being in a "Preserved Tablet" (لوح محفوظ), that it is an "August Book" which falsehood cannot approach, that it is a "decisive word," a "guidance," a "light," a "Criterion" (فرقان) —*the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood*— between truth and falsehood, and a "miraculous sign"?

Can we say that these verses, despite their abundance and the fact that their context (سياق) resists restriction, are restricted to only a part? That only *some* of the Book—the part not forgotten or abrogated in recitation—is that which falsehood cannot approach, and is a decisive word, guidance, light, criterion, and eternal miracle?

Is making a speech "abrogated in recitation" and "consigned to oblivion" (نسياً منسياً) anything other than invalidating and killing it? Is the turning of a beneficial word into something that can never benefit and can never rectify any matter anything other than its cancellation, rejection, and neglect? And how can this be reconciled with the Quran being a "Reminder" (ذكر)?

The truth is that the narrations of alteration narrated from the paths of both sects, as well as the narrations regarding the abrogation of the recitation of some Quranic verses, contradict the Book with a definitive contradiction (مخالفة قطعية).

The answer to the fourth point: The core of the reports necessitating the similarity of the events occurring in this nation to what occurred among the Children of Israel is beyond doubt; they are widely transmitted (متظافرة) or even mass-transmitted (متواترة). However, these narrations do not indicate similarity in every single respect; this is obvious, and necessity (الضرورة) rejects it.

What is meant by similarity is similarity in general (في الجملة) in terms of results and effects. Thus, it is possible that the similarity of this nation to the Children of Israel in the issue of the "alteration of the Book" lies only in the occurrence of disagreement and division within the nation, its branching into various sects that declare one another disbelievers, and its splitting into seventy-three sects—just as the Christians split into seventy-two and the Jews into seventy-one. This meaning has been mentioned in many of these narrations, to the point that some have claimed they are mass-transmitted.

It is well known that all of them rely on the Book of Allah for what they have chosen, and this is only due to "distorting words from their contexts" (تحريف الكلم عن مواضعه), interpreting the Noble Quran by personal opinion (التفسير بالرأي), and relying on reports narrated concerning the exegesis of verses without weighing them against the Book to distinguish the sound from the sick.

In summary, the core of the narrations indicating similarity between the two nations does not indicate any of the "alteration" they claim. Yes, in some of them, alteration by change and omission is mentioned, but this category—despite its infirmity—contradicts the Book, as previously stated.

## Chapter 4

In the *History* of al-Ya‘qubi: ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab said to Abu Bakr: "O Successor of the Messenger of Allah, most of the carriers of the Quran were killed on the day of al-Yamamah. If you were to collect the Quran, for I fear that its carriers will perish."
