Abu Bakr said to him: "Shall I do something the Messenger of Allah did not do?" ‘Umar kept at him until he collected it and wrote it in sheets (صحف); it had been scattered on palm stalks and other materials.

He seated twenty-five men from the Quraysh and fifty men from the Ansar and said: "Write the Quran and present it to Sa‘id ibn al-‘As, for he is an eloquent man."

Some narrated: That ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام) had collected it when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away—and he brought it carrying it on a camel, saying: "This is the Quran, I have collected it." He said: He had divided it into seven parts, then he mentioned the parts.

In the *History* of Abu al-Fida’: A group of reciters (القراء) from the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار) were killed in the battle against Musaylimah. When Abu Bakr saw the large number of those killed, he ordered the collection of the Quran from the mouths of men, palm stalks, and skins. That written material was left with Hafsah bint ‘Umar, the wife of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). (End quote).

The basis for what they both mentioned is found in the narrations. Al-Bukhari recorded in his *Sahih* from Zayd ibn Thabit, who said: "Abu Bakr sent for me after the slaughter of the people of al-Yamamah, and ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab was with him. Abu Bakr said: ‘Umar came to me and said: The slaughter has intensified among the reciters of the Quran, and I fear that the slaughter will intensify among the reciters in other places, and thus much of the Quran will be lost. I believe you should order the collection of the Quran. I said to ‘Umar: How can we do something the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not do? ‘Umar said: By Allah, this is good. He kept reviewing it with me until Allah opened my chest to that and I saw what ‘Umar saw.’"

Zayd said: "Abu Bakr said: You are a wise young man, we do not suspect you, and you used to write the revelation (الوحي) for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). So track the Quran and collect it. By Allah, if they had tasked me with moving one of the mountains, it would not have been heavier upon me than what he ordered me regarding the collection of the Quran. I said: How can you two do something the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not do? He said: By Allah, it is good."

"Abu Bakr kept reviewing it with me until Allah opened my chest for that which He had opened the chests of Abu Bakr and ‘Umar. So I tracked the Quran, collecting it from palm stalks (العسف), thin white stones (اللخاف), and the chests of men. I found the end of Surah al-Tawbah with Khuzaymah al-Ansari; I did not find it with anyone else: **لَقَدْ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ** — 'There has certainly come to you an Apostle' — until the conclusion of *Bara’ah*. The sheets remained with Abu Bakr until Allah took him, then with ‘Umar during his life, then with Hafsah bint ‘Umar."

From Ibn Abi Dawud, via Yahya ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Hatib, who said: "‘Umar came and said: Whoever received anything of the Quran from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), let him bring it. They used to write that on sheets, tablets, and palm stalks. He would not accept anything from anyone until two witnesses testified."

Also from him, via Hisham ibn ‘Urwah from his father—and there is a break in the chain: "Abu Bakr said to ‘Umar and Zayd: Sit at the door of the mosque—whoever comes to you with two witnesses for something from the Book of Allah, write it down."

In *al-Itqan*, from Ibn Ashtah in *al-Masahif*, from al-Layth ibn Sa‘d, who said: "The first to collect the Quran was Abu Bakr, and Zayd wrote it. People would come to Zayd ibn Thabit—and he would not write a verse except with two just witnesses (شاهدي عدل). The end of Surah *Bara’ah* was not found except with Abu Khuzaymah ibn Thabit—so he said: Write it, for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) made his testimony equal to the testimony of two men—so he wrote it. ‘Umar brought the 'Verse of Stoning' but he did not write it because he was alone."

From Ibn Abi Dawud in *al-Masahif*, via Muhammad ibn Ishaq from Yahya ibn ‘Abbad ibn ‘Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr from his father, who said: "al-Harith ibn Khuzaymah brought me these two verses from the end of Surah *Bara’ah* and said: I bear witness that I heard them from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and memorized them. ‘Umar said: And I bear witness that I heard them. Then he said: If they were three verses, I would have made them a separate chapter—so look for the end of a chapter of the Quran and attach them to its end."

Also from him, via Abu al-‘Aliyah from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b: "They collected the Quran, and when they reached the verse in Surah *Bara’ah*: **ثُمَّ انْصَرَفُوا صَرَفَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ** — 'Then they leave. Allah has turned away their hearts because they are a people who do not understand' — they thought this was the last of what was revealed. Ubayy said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited to me two verses after this: **لَقَدْ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ** — 'There has certainly come to you an Apostle' — to the end of the chapter."

In *al-Itqan*, from al-Dayr ‘Aquli in his *Fawa’id*: Ibrahim ibn Yasar narrated to us, Sufyan ibn ‘Uyaynah narrated to us, from al-Zuhri, from ‘Ubayd, from Zayd ibn Thabit, who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away and the Quran had not been collected into anything."

In the *Mustadrak* of al-Hakim, with his chain to Zayd ibn Thabit, who said: "We were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) assembling (نؤلف) the Quran from scraps (الرقاع)..." (the hadith).

I say: Perhaps what is meant is joining some verses revealed piecemeal to certain chapters, or attaching some chapters to others that are similar in category, such as the long ones (الطوال), the hundred-verse ones (المئين), and the short ones (المفصلات), as mention of them has appeared in the Prophetic traditions. Otherwise, the assembly of the Quran and its collection into a single volume (مصحف) only occurred after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away, without any doubt. What follows should be understood in this light.

In the *Sahih* of al-Nasa’i, from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: "I collected the Quran and recited it every night—this reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and he said: Recite it in a month."

In *al-Itqan*, from Ibn Abi Dawud with a good chain from Muhammad ibn Ka‘b al-Qurazi, who said: "Five of the Ansar collected the Quran during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, ‘Ubadah ibn al-Samit, Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, Abu al-Darda’, and Abu Ayyub al-Ansari."

In it, from al-Bayhaqi in *al-Madkhal* from Ibn Sirin, who said: "Four collected the Quran during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about whom there is no disagreement—Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, and Abu Zayd—and they disagreed about two men out of three: Abu al-Darda’ and ‘Uthman." It was also said: ‘Uthman and Tamim al-Dari.

In it, from him and from Ibn Abi Dawud from al-Sha‘bi, who said: "Seven collected the Quran during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): Ubayy, Zayd, Mu‘adh, Abu al-Darda’, Sa‘id ibn ‘Ubayd, Abu Zayd, and Mujammi‘ ibn Harithah, who had taken all of it except two or three chapters."

In it also, from Ibn Ashtah in the book *al-Masahif* via Kahmas from Ibn Buraydah, who said: "The first to collect the Quran into a volume (مصحف) was Salim, the client of Abu Hudhayfah—he swore he would not put on his cloak until he collected it, so he collected it." (The hadith).

I say: The most these narrations indicate is their mere collection of the chapters and verses that were revealed; as for the attention to the order of chapters and verses as it is today, or any other order, they do not indicate that. This was the first collection during the time of Abu Bakr.

## Chapter 5

The Quran was collected a second time during the era of ‘Uthman when the volumes (المصاحف) differed and the recitations (القراءات) multiplied.

Al-Ya‘qubi said in his *History*: "‘Uthman collected the Quran and assembled it, placing the long chapters with the long ones and the short chapters with the short ones. He wrote regarding the collection of the volumes from the horizons until they were gathered, then he scalded them with hot water and vinegar. It was said: He burned them, and no volume remained except that he did that to it, save for the volume of Ibn Mas‘ud."

Ibn Mas‘ud was in Kufa and refused to hand over his volume to ‘Abd Allah ibn ‘Amir. ‘Uthman wrote [to him]: "Send him to me, for if this religion is not a confusion (خبال) and this nation a corruption..." He entered the mosque while ‘Uthman was preaching. ‘Uthman said: "An evil beast has come to you." He spoke harsh words to Ibn Mas‘ud, and ‘Uthman ordered him to be dragged by his leg until two of his ribs were broken. ‘A’ishah spoke out and said much.

He sent them to the provinces; he sent a volume to Kufa, a volume to Basra, a volume to Medina, a volume to Mecca, a volume to Egypt, a volume to Syria, a volume to Bahrain, a volume to Yemen, and a volume to al-Jazirah.

He ordered the people to recite according to one version. The reason for this was that he heard people saying: "The Quran of the family of so-and-so," so he wanted his version to be the only one. It was said: Ibn Mas‘ud had written to him about that, but when he heard that he was burning the volumes, he said: "I did not intend this." It was said: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman wrote to him about that. (End of the relevant portion).

In *al-Itqan*, al-Bukhari narrated from Anas: "Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman came to ‘Uthman—he had been campaigning with the people of Syria in the conquest of Armenia and Azerbaijan along with the people of Iraq—and their disagreement in recitation alarmed Hudhayfah. He said to ‘Uthman: O Commander of the Faithful, save this nation before they differ like the Jews and Christians. So he sent to Hafsah: Send us the sheets—we will copy them into the volumes then return them to you. Hafsah sent them to ‘Uthman, and he ordered Zayd ibn Thabit, ‘Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr, Sa‘id ibn al-‘As, and ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Harith ibn Hisham—and they copied them into the volumes."

‘Uthman said to the three Qurayshi men: "If you and Zayd ibn Thabit differ regarding anything of the Quran—write it in the tongue of Quraysh, for it was only revealed in their tongue." They did so until they had copied the sheets into the volumes. ‘Uthman returned the sheets to Hafsah and sent to every horizon a volume from what they had copied, and he ordered that whatever else of the Quran was in any sheet or volume be burned.

Zayd said: "A verse from *al-Ahzab* when we were copying the volume—I used to hear the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reciting it—we searched for it and found it with Khuzaymah ibn Thabit al-Ansari: **مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا مَا عَاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ** — 'Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah' — so we attached it to its chapter in the volume."

In it, Ibn Ashtah recorded via Ayyub from Abu Qilabah, who said: "A man from the Banu ‘Amir called Anas ibn Malik narrated to me: They differed regarding the Quran during the time of ‘Uthman—until the youths and teachers fought one another. This reached ‘Uthman ibn ‘Affan and he said: Here with me you deny it and make mistakes in it—so those far from me must be even more denying and making more mistakes. O Companions of Muhammad, gather and write for the people a leading version (إماماً)."

So they gathered, and when they differed and disputed over a verse, they would say: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this to so-and-so." They would send for him while he was three days' journey from Medina, and it would be said to him: "How did the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recite such-and-such verse to you?" He would say: "Such-and-such," and they would write it, having left a space for it.

In it, from Ibn Abi Dawud via Ibn Sirin from Kathir ibn Aflah, who said: "When ‘Uthman wanted to write the volumes—he gathered twelve men for it from the Quraysh and the Ansar. They sent for the chest (الربعة) that was in the house of ‘Umar and it was brought—and ‘Uthman would supervise them. When they disputed over something, they would delay it."

Muhammad said: "I thought they only delayed it to see who among them was most recent in time with the 'Final Presentation' (العرضة الأخيرة) so they could write it according to his word."

In it, Ibn Abi Dawud recorded with an authentic chain from Suwayd ibn Ghaflah, who said: "‘Ali said: Do not say anything but good about ‘Uthman—for by Allah, he did not do what he did with the volumes except in consultation with a group of us. He said: What do you say about these recitations? For it has reached me that some say: 'My recitation is better than yours,' and this is close to disbelief. We said: What do you see? [He said: I see] that the people be gathered upon one volume—so there will be no division or disagreement. We said: What an excellent idea."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Durays recorded from ‘Ilba’ ibn Ahmar that ‘Uthman ibn ‘Affan: "When he wanted to write the volumes—they wanted to drop the 'and' (الواو) that is in *Bara’ah*: **وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ** — 'And those who treasure up gold and silver'. Ubayy said: You shall attach it or I shall place my sword upon my shoulder. So they attached it."

In *al-Itqan*, from Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Hibban, and al-Hakim from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "I said to ‘Uthman: What prompted you to take *al-Anfal*, which is from the *Mathani* —*chapters with fewer than a hundred verses*— and *Bara’ah*, which is from the *Mi’in* —*chapters with about a hundred verses*— and put them together, without writing between them the line 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful,' and placing them among the 'Seven Long Ones' (السبع الطوال)?"

‘Uthman said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to have chapters of many verses revealed to him. When something was revealed to him, he would call some of those who used to write and say: 'Place these verses in the chapter in which such-and-such is mentioned.' *al-Anfal* was among the first of what was revealed in Medina, and *Bara’ah* was among the last of the Quran in revelation. Its story was similar to its story, so I thought it was part of it. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed away and did not clarify to us that it was part of it."

"For that reason, I paired them together and did not write between them the line 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful,' and I placed them among the Seven Long Ones."

I say: The "Seven Long Ones"—according to what appears from this narration and is also narrated from Abu Jubayr—are *al-Baqarah*, *Al ‘Imran*, *al-Nisa’*, *al-Ma’idah*, *al-An‘am*, *al-A‘raf*, and *Yunus*. They were placed in the first collection according to this order, then ‘Uthman changed this order; he took *al-Anfal*, which is from the *Mathani*, and *Bara’ah*, which is from the *Mi’in*, before the *Mathani*, and placed them between *al-A‘raf* and *Yunus*, putting *al-Anfal* before *Bara’ah*.

## Chapter 6

The narrations cited in the previous two chapters are the most famous reports regarding the collection and assembly of the Quran, ranging from authentic to weak. They indicate that the first collection was a gathering of the scattered chapters written on palm stalks, thin stones, shoulder blades, skins, and scraps, and the attachment of verses revealed separately to chapters that suited them.

The second collection—the ‘Uthmanic collection—was the returning of the volumes that had spread from the first collection (after the occurrence of conflicting versions and differing recitations) to a single agreed-upon volume. This is except for Zayd's statement that he attached his saying: **مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا مَا عَاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ** — "Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah" — to Surah *al-Ahzab* in the volume; thus, the volumes had been recited for fifteen years without this verse.

Al-Bukhari narrated from Ibn al-Zubayr, who said: "I said to ‘Uthman: **وَالَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنْكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَاجاً** — 'Those of you who die leaving wives' — has been abrogated by the other verse, so why do you write it or leave it? He said: O son of my brother, I will not change anything from its place."

What free investigation into these narrations and their indications—which are the mainstay of this subject—yields is that they are solitary reports (آحاد), not mass-transmitted (متواترة). However, they are surrounded by definitive indications. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to convey to the people what was revealed to him from his Lord without concealing any of it. He used to teach them and clarify for them what was revealed to them from their Lord, as the Quran explicitly states. A group of them never ceased teaching and learning the Quran—both its recitation and its explanation; these were the reciters (القراء), a vast number of whom were killed in the expedition of al-Yamamah.

The people had an intense desire to take and engage with the Quran. This matter was never abandoned, nor did the Quran disappear from among them for even a day or part of a day, until it was collected into a single volume and then agreed upon. Thus, the Quran was not afflicted with what afflicted the Torah, the Gospel, and the books of other prophets.

Add to this the countless narrations from both Shi‘a and Sunni paths regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recitation of many Quranic chapters in the daily obligatory prayers and elsewhere in the hearing of the public. In these narrations, a vast number of Quranic chapters—both Meccan and Medinan—are named.

Add to this what was previously mentioned in the narration of ‘Uthman ibn Abi al-‘As regarding the exegesis of His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness" (16:90), from his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "Gabriel came to me with this verse and ordered me to place it in its position in the chapter." Similar in indication to this narration is what points to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recitation of some chapters revealed piecemeal, such as *Al ‘Imran*, *al-Nisa’*, and others; this indicates that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to order the scribes of revelation to attach certain verses to their proper places.

The greatest of definitive proofs is what was mentioned at the beginning of these discussions: that the Quran in our hands possesses the noble attributes with which Allah, the Exalted, described it.

In summary, what these narrations indicate is: First, that what is contained between the two covers of the Quran is the Word of Allah, the Exalted; nothing has been added to it, and nothing has been changed. As for omission (النقص), these reports do not suffice to definitively negate it, as it is narrated through several paths that ‘Umar used to frequently mention the "Verse of Stoning" but it was not written from him. As for their interpreting this narration and all else that has been reported regarding alteration—and al-Alusi mentioned in his exegesis that they are beyond counting—as "abrogation of recitation," you have already seen its falsity and realized that proving "abrogation of recitation" is more hideous than proving the principle of alteration itself.

Furthermore, those who had a volume other than what Zayd collected (first by order of Abu Bakr and second by order of ‘Uthman)—such as ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام), Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, and ‘Abd Allah ibn Mas‘ud—did not deny anything contained in the circulating volume. This is except for what was transmitted from Ibn Mas‘ud, that he did not write the two "Seeking Refuge" chapters (المعوذتين) in his volume, saying they were two formulas of refuge (عوذتان) brought by Gabriel to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to seek refuge for al-Hasan and al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام). The rest of the Companions rejected this, and the texts from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are mass-transmitted that they are two chapters of the Quran.

In short, the previous narrations—as you see—are solitary reports surrounded by definitive indications that negate alteration by addition and change definitively, but negate omission only probabilistically (ظناً). The claim of some that there is mass-transmission (تواتر) regarding all three aspects has no basis.

Reliance in this matter is upon the argument we presented at the beginning of these discussions: that the Quran in our hands possesses the noble attributes by which Allah, the Glorified, described the actual Quran He revealed to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—such as its being a "decisive word," a "resolver of disagreement," a "reminder," a "guide," a "light," and a "clarifier" of true knowledge and primordial laws (الشرائع الفطرية), and a "miraculous sign," among its other noble attributes.

It is appropriate that we rely on this aspect, for the Quran's proof of being the Word of Allah revealed to His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is itself, characterized by those noble attributes, without depending on any other matter beyond itself, whatever it may be. Its proof is with it wherever it is realized, in whoever's hand it may be, and by whatever path it arrived.

In other words, the Quran revealed from Allah to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) does not depend, in being characterized by its noble attributes, upon the proof of its attribution to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through mass-transmitted or widely-transmitted reports—even if it possesses that. Rather, the matter is the opposite: its being characterized by its noble attributes is the proof of its attribution. It is not like books and treatises attributed to authors and writers, or sayings transmitted from scholars and thinkers, whose correct attribution depends on definitive transmission and reaching the level of mass-transmission or wide-circulation, for instance. Rather, its very essence (ذاته) is the proof of its established status.

Second: The order of the chapters (السور) is from the Companions in the first and second collections. Evidence for this is what was mentioned in the narrations regarding ‘Uthman's placement of *al-Anfal* and *Bara’ah* between *al-A‘raf* and *Yunus*, whereas they were later in the first collection.

Further evidence is what has been reported regarding the difference in the order of the volumes of other Companions compared to both the first and second collections. For instance, it is narrated that the volume of ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) was arranged according to the order of revelation (ترتيب النزول); its beginning was *Iqra’*, then *al-Muddaththir*, then *Nun*, then *al-Muzzammil*, then *Tabbat*, then *al-Takwir*, and so on to the end of the Meccan and Medinan chapters. This was transmitted in *al-Itqan* from Ibn Faris, while in the *History* of al-Ya‘qubi, there is another order for his (عليه‌السلام) volume.

It was transmitted from Ibn Ashtah in *al-Masahif* with his chain to Abu Ja‘far al-Kufi regarding the order of Ubayy's volume, which differs significantly from the circulating volume. Similarly, from him with his chain to Jarir ibn ‘Abd al-Hamid regarding the order of ‘Abd Allah ibn Mas‘ud's volume, starting with the long ones, then the hundred-verse ones, then the *Mathani*, then the *Mufassal*; this also differs from the circulating volume.

Many of them have held that the order of the chapters is "divinely ordained" (توقيفي) and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the one who ordered this arrangement by an indication from Gabriel by the command of Allah, the Glorified. Some even went to extremes, claiming this is established by mass-transmission. I wonder, where is this mass-transmission when the mainstay of the narrations on this subject has been presented and there is no trace of this meaning in them? The argument of some for this, based on what has been narrated regarding the Quran's revelation from the Preserved Tablet to the lowest heaven as a whole and then to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) gradually, will be discussed later.

Third: The placement of some Quranic verses that were revealed separately in their current positions was not free from the intervention of the Companions through personal judgment (الاجتهاد), as is apparent from the narrations of the first collection previously mentioned.

As for the narration of ‘Uthman ibn Abi al-‘As from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Gabriel came to me and ordered me to place this verse in this position in the chapter: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** — 'Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness'..." it does not indicate more than his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) action regarding *some* verses in general (في الجملة), not all of them (بالجملة). Even if we were to accept it, the narrations in our hands do not indicate that the Companions' arrangement matched his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrangement. Mere good faith in them does not allow the narrations to carry an indication they do not possess; it only suggests they would not have intentionally contradicted his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrangement in what they knew, not in what they were ignorant of. In the narrations of the first collection, there are clear proofs that they were not knowledgeable of the positions of all verses, nor of the verses themselves.

This is supported by the widely-circulated reports (المستفيضة) narrated from both Shi‘a and Sunni paths that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers only knew the completion of a chapter by the revelation of the *Basmalah*. This was narrated by Abu Dawud, al-Hakim, al-Bayhaqi, and al-Bazzar via Sa‘id ibn Jubayr—as in *al-Itqan*—from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not know the separation (فضل) of a chapter until 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful' was revealed to him." al-Bazzar added: "When it was revealed, he knew that the chapter had ended and another had been received or begun."

Also from al-Hakim through another way from Sa‘id from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The Muslims did not know the conclusion of a chapter until 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful' was revealed. When it was revealed, they knew the chapter had concluded." Its chain is according to the criteria of the two Sheikhs (al-Bukhari and Muslim).

Also from him through another way from Sa‘id from Ibn ‘Abbas: "When Gabriel came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and recited 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful,' he knew it was a chapter." Its chain is authentic.

I say: Something close to this has been narrated in several other reports, and it has been narrated from Shi‘a paths from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام).

The narrations—as you see—are explicit in their indication that the verses were arranged with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to the order of revelation. Thus, Meccan verses were in Meccan chapters and Medinan verses in Medinan chapters, unless one assumes a chapter where some was revealed in Mecca and some in Medina—and this assumption is only realized in a single chapter.

The necessary consequence of this is that the variation we observe in the positions of verses is based on the personal judgment (اجتهاد) of the Companions.

To clarify: there are countless narrations regarding the "occasions of revelation" (أسباب النزول) indicating that many verses in Medinan chapters were revealed in Mecca and vice versa, and that verses of the Quran revealed, for instance, at the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era are located in chapters revealed at the beginning of the Hijrah, while many other chapters were revealed between the two times. An example is Surah al-Baqarah, which was revealed in the first year of the Hijrah, yet contains the verses of usury (الربا), which reports state were among the last things revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—to the point that it is narrated from ‘Umar that he said: "The Messenger of Allah died and did not clarify the verses of usury to us." It also contains His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ** — "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (2:281), which is reported to be the very last thing revealed of the Quran to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

These verses revealed separately and placed in chapters that do not match them in being Meccan or Medinan are placed out of their order according to the sequence of revelation, and this is only due to the judgment of the Companions.

This is supported by what is in *al-Itqan* from Ibn Hajar: "It has been narrated from ‘Ali that he collected the Quran according to the order of revelation immediately after the death of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)." This was recorded by Ibn Abi Dawud and is among the accepted meanings of Shi‘a narrations.

This is what the literal sense of the previous narrations on this subject indicates. However, the majority (الجمهور) have insisted that the order of the verses is divinely ordained (توقيفي); thus, the verses of the circulating volume today—the ‘Uthmanic volume—are arranged as the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arranged them by an indication from Gabriel. They interpreted the literal sense of the narrations by saying that the Companions' collection was not a collection of arrangement, but rather a gathering of the chapters and their arranged verses that they knew and had memorized from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), between two covers and in one place.

You are well aware that the manner of the first collection indicated by the narrations explicitly refutes this claim.

Sometimes this is argued for by what some have claimed as consensus (الإجماع) on the matter. al-Suyuti in *al-Itqan* quoted al-Zarkashi's claim of consensus, and from Abu Ja‘far ibn al-Zubayr the negation of any disagreement among Muslims regarding it. This is a "transmitted consensus" (إجماع منقول) that cannot be relied upon given the existence of disagreement regarding the principle of alteration and the indication of the previous narrations to the contrary.

Sometimes it is argued for by mass-transmission (التواتر), and this is found in the words of many who claimed the mass-transmission of the existing arrangement from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is strange. In *al-Itqan*, after quoting what al-Bukhari and others narrated through several paths from Anas that he said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) died and none collected the Quran except four: Abu al-Darda’, Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, Zayd ibn Thabit, and Abu Zayd" (and in one narration: "Ubayy ibn Ka‘b" instead of Abu al-Darda’), it is quoted from al-Maziri that he said: "A group of heretics (الملاحدة) have clung to this statement of Anas, but they have no basis in it. We do not accept taking it according to its literal sense. Even if we did, how do they know that the reality of the matter was such? Even if we accepted that, it does not follow from the fact that each individual of a vast multitude did not memorize all of it that the collective of that vast multitude did not memorize it. It is not a condition of mass-transmission that every individual memorize all of it; rather, if the whole memorized the whole—even if distributed—it suffices." (End quote).

As for his claim that the literal meaning of Anas's words is not intended, it is something not listened to in linguistic investigations based on the literal sense of the word, except with an indication from the speaker's own words or what stands in their place. Mere claim and reliance on the words of others is not enough.

Furthermore, if Anas's words were taken contrary to their literal sense, it would be necessary to interpret them as meaning that these four only collected the *bulk* of the Quran and most of its chapters and verses during the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era—not that they and other Companions collected the *entire* Quran as it is in the ‘Uthmanic volume, memorized the order of its chapters and verses, and precisely recorded the position of every single one of them to the last. Here is Zayd ibn Thabit himself—one of the four mentioned in Anas's hadith and the one in charge of both the first and second collections—explicitly stating in his narrations that he had not memorized all the verses.

Similar to this is what is in *al-Itqan* from Ibn Ashtah in *al-Masahif* with an authentic chain from Muhammad ibn Sirin, who said: "Abu Bakr died and had not collected the Quran, and ‘Umar was killed and had not collected the Quran."

As for his saying: "Even if we accepted it, how do they know that the reality of the matter was such?" it is turned back upon him: how does this speaker know that the reality of the matter is as he claims, when you have seen the evidence to the contrary?

As for his saying that it suffices for the realization of mass-transmission that the whole memorized all of the Quran in a distributed manner, it is a clear fallacy. This only yields the fact that the *totality* of the Quran, as a whole, is transmitted through mass-transmission. As for every single one of the Quranic verses being preserved in terms of its place and position through mass-transmission, it does not—and this is obvious.

In *al-Itqan*, it is quoted from al-Baghawi that he said in *Sharh al-Sunnah*: "The Companions collected the Quran between two covers—which Allah revealed to His Messenger—without adding or subtracting anything from it, for fear of some of it being lost with the passing of its memorizers. They wrote it as they heard it from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), without putting anything forward or backward, or establishing for it an arrangement they did not take from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)."

"The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to dictate to his Companions and teach them what was revealed to him of the Quran according to the arrangement that is now in our volumes, by Gabriel's informing him of that and his informing him at the revelation of every verse that 'this verse is to be written after such-and-such verse in such-and-such chapter.'"

"Thus it is established that the Companions' effort was in collecting it in one place, not in its arrangement. For the Quran is written in the Preserved Tablet according to this arrangement; Allah revealed it as a whole to the lowest heaven, then He revealed it piecemeal as needed—and the order of revelation is different from the order of recitation." (End quote).

It is quoted from Ibn al-Hassar that he said: "The arrangement of the chapters and the placing of verses in their positions was only by revelation. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to say: 'Place such-and-such verse in such-and-such position.' Certainty has been obtained from mass-transmitted reports of this arrangement from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Companions only agreed on placing it thus in the volume." (End quote). Something close to this was also quoted from a group of others such as al-Bayhaqi, al-Tibi, and Ibn Hajar.

As for their saying that the Companions only wrote the volume according to the arrangement they took from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) without contradicting him in anything, it is something not indicated by any of the previous narrations. What is accepted from their indication is only that they established what was proven by evidence from the text of the verses; there is no indication in that to the manner of arranging verses revealed separately—and this is obvious. Yes, in the previous narration of Ibn ‘Abbas from ‘Uthman, there is an indication of that, except that what is in it is that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to order *some* scribes of revelation to do that, which is different from his informing *all* the Companions of it. Furthermore, the narration is contradicted by the narrations of the first collection and the reports of the revelation of the *Basmalah* and others.

As for their saying that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) dictated this arrangement existing in our volumes to the Companions by a divinely ordained instruction from Gabriel and heavenly revelation, it seems to be an allusion to the previously mentioned hadith of ‘Uthman ibn Abi al-‘As regarding the verse: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness." You have already seen that it is a single hadith regarding the specific position of a single verse; where is that in relation to the positions of all the separate verses?

As for their saying that the Quran is written according to this arrangement in the Preserved Tablet, and that Allah revealed it to the lowest heaven then revealed it piecemeal as needed, etc., it is an allusion to what has been widely narrated from both Shi‘a and Sunni paths regarding the Quran's revelation as a whole from the Preserved Tablet to the lowest heaven, then its revelation from there piecemeal to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). However, the narrations do not contain the slightest indication that the Quran was written in the Preserved Tablet or organized in the lowest heaven according to the arrangement existing in the volume we have—and this is obvious.

Furthermore, the discussion regarding the meaning of the Quran's writing in the Preserved Tablet and its revelation to the lowest heaven will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on the relevant verses, such as the beginning of Surahs al-Zukhruf and al-Dukhan, and Surah al-Qadr.

As for their saying that certainty has been obtained through mass-transmitted reports from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding this arrangement existing in the volumes, you have seen that it is a claim devoid of evidence and that this mass-transmission...

There is no report concerning him regarding every single verse. How could there be, when narrations have multiplied stating that Ibn Mas'ud did not write the two surahs of seeking refuge (المعوذتين) — *Surah al-Falaq and Surah al-Nas* — in his codex (مصحف), and he used to say they were not part of the Quran, but rather Gabriel descended with them as a protection (تعويذا) for the two grandsons (الحسنين) — *Imam al-Hasan and Imam al-Husayn (peace be upon them)*? He used to erase them from the codices, and it has not been transmitted that he ever recanted his position. How then could such a mass-transmitted (تواتر) fact remain hidden from him throughout his life after the first compilation?

## Section 7

Related to the previous discussion is the inquiry into the narrations of causing to forget (الإنساء) — to which a general reference has already been made. These are several narrations reported through Sunni (أهل السنة) channels regarding the abrogation (نسخ) and causing to forget (إنساء) of the Quran, upon which they have based the narrations of distortion (التحريف) involving omission (سقوط) and alteration (تغيير).

Among them is what is in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim in *al-Kuna*, Ibn Adi, and Ibn Asakir, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Among that which used to be revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was revelation (الوحي) by night — and he would forget it by day. So Allah revealed: **مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا** 'For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or one like it.' (2:106)"

Also therein, from Abu Dawud in his *al-Nasikh* and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* from Abu Umamah: That a group of the Helpers (الأنصار) from the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله informed him — that a man stood in the middle of the night intending to begin a surah — which he had memorized, but he could not recall any of it — except 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful' (بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم). This happened to several of his companions — so in the morning they asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the surah — and he remained silent for a while, returning nothing to them. Then he said: "It was abrogated (نسخت) last night." Thus it was erased from their hearts and from everything it was contained in.

I say: The story is narrated through several chains with wordings that are close in meaning (مضمونا).

Also therein, from Abd al-Razzaq, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Abu Dawud in his *al-Nasikh*, his son in *al-Masahif*, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it), from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas: That he read: (ما ننسخ من آية أو ننسأها). It was said to him: "Indeed, Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib reads it as (نُنْسِهَا)." Sa'd replied: "The Quran was not revealed to al-Musayyib nor the family of al-Musayyib. Allah said: **سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تَنْسَى** 'We shall have you recite, and you will not forget' (87:6) and **وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ** 'and remember your Lord when you forget' (18:24)."

I say: By adhering to these two verses, he intends to show that Allah removed forgetfulness (النسيان) from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Therefore, it becomes necessary to read it as (ننسأها) from postponement (النسيء) — *the act of delaying or leaving aside* — in the sense of leaving and delaying. Thus, the meaning of His saying: **مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ** "For any verse that We abrogate" would be the removal of the verse from practical application (العمل) but not from recitation (التلاوة), such as the verse of the secret talk charity (آية صدقة النجوى). And by His saying: (أو ننسأها), it would mean leaving the verse and removing it from them entirely, abolishing it from both application and recitation, as its interpretation (تفسير) has been narrated from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Qatadah, and others.

Also therein, Ibn al-Anbari extracted from Abu Dhibyan who said: Ibn Abbas said to us: "Which of the two recitations (القراءتين) do you consider the first?" We said: "The recitation of Abdullah, and our recitation is the latter one." He said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to have the Quran reviewed (يعرض) with him by Gabriel once every year in the month of Ramadan — and he reviewed it with him twice in the final year — so Abdullah witnessed from it what was abrogated (نسخ) and what was changed (بدل)."

I say: This meaning is narrated through other chains from Ibn Abbas, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud himself, and others among the companions (الصحابة) and the followers (التابعين), and there are other narrations regarding causing to forget (الإنساء).

The summary of what is gathered from them is that abrogation (النسخ) may occur in the ruling (الحكم), like the abrogated verses (الآيات المنسوخة) that remain fixed in the codex (المصحف). It may also occur in the recitation (التلاوة) along with the abrogation of its ruling, or without the abrogation of its ruling. It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying: **مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ** (Al-Baqarah: 106), and it will come under His saying: **وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَا آيَةً مَكَانَ آيَةٍ** (Al-Nahl: 101), that both verses are foreign to the concept of causing to forget (الإنساء) in the sense of abrogating the recitation (نسخ التلاوة). It has also been mentioned in the previous sections that these narrations contradict the explicit text of the Book (صريح الكتاب); therefore, the proper course is to categorize them with the narrations of distortion (التحريف) and discard both types altogether.
